risky_values:
SSLv3
TLSv1
TLSv1_2016
TLSv1.1_2016
TLSv1.2_2018
TLSv1.2_2019
risk_reason: CloudFront security policy controls both the minimum viewer SSL/TLS protocol and supported ciphers. Old policies allow SSLv3/TLS 1.0/TLS 1.1 or legacy ciphers such as 3DES/RC4/CBC/SHA.
security_value_reason: Use the newest CloudFront viewer security policies that keep the minimum protocol at TLS 1.2 or TLS 1.3 and avoid legacy cipher exposure.
risk_basis:
Risk rule 1: cipher name does not contain ECDHE or TLS_, so it lacks forward secrecy (FS).
Risk rule 2: CBC mode, non-AEAD. AEAD examples are ChaCha20 and GCM. CBC has known attack history and is not recommended even when not fully broken in this context.
Risk rule 3: SHA means SHA-1 style legacy hash naming, not SHA256/SHA384/SHA512, and is treated as risky.

recommended_values:
https-only
redirect-to-https
risky_values: allow-all for authenticated/API/sensitive traffic
risk_reason: Allowing HTTP leaves the viewer-to-CloudFront segment unencrypted and exposes credentials, cookies, tokens, or request payloads to interception or modification.
security_value_reason: Use https-only for sensitive workloads; redirect-to-https can be used for public static compatibility.

recommended_values:
https-only
OriginSslProtocols: TLSv1.2
risky_values:
http-only
match-viewer when viewer HTTP is allowed
OriginSslProtocols: TLSv1
OriginSslProtocols: TLSv1.1
risk_reason: CloudFront-to-origin traffic remains exposed if the origin policy allows HTTP or old TLS versions.
security_value_reason: Use HTTPS-only to complete end-to-end encryption from viewer through CloudFront to origin.

recommended_values:
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-3-2021-06
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-3-PQ-2025-09
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-Res-2021-06
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-Res-PQ-2025-09
risky_values:
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-2021-06
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-PQ-2025-09
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-Ext2-2021-06
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-Ext2-PQ-2025-09
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-Ext1-2021-06
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-Ext1-PQ-2025-09
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-1-2021-06
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-0-2021-06
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-0-PQ-2025-09
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS-1-2-Ext-2018-06
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS-1-2-2017-01
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS-1-1-2017-01
ELBSecurityPolicy-2016-08
risk_reason: Policies in the risk column either support TLS 1.0/1.1 or include ciphers matching the weak-cipher rules: no ECDHE/TLS_ forward secrecy indicator, CBC mode, or legacy SHA naming.
security_value_reason: Recommended TLS policies are TLS 1.3 only or Res policies with TLS 1.2/1.3 and AEAD/forward-secrecy-focused cipher profiles. AWS recommends the PQ-TLS policy ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-Res-PQ-2025-09 for transition to post-quantum TLS.
risk_basis:
Risk rule 1: cipher name does not contain ECDHE or TLS_, so it lacks forward secrecy (FS).
Risk rule 2: CBC mode, non-AEAD. AEAD examples are ChaCha20 and GCM. CBC has known attack history and is not recommended even when not fully broken in this context.
Risk rule 3: SHA means SHA-1 style legacy hash naming, not SHA256/SHA384/SHA512, and is treated as risky.

recommended_values:
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-3-FIPS-2023-04
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-3-FIPS-PQ-2025-09
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-Res-FIPS-2023-04
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-Res-FIPS-PQ-2025-09
risky_values:
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-FIPS-2023-04
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-FIPS-PQ-2025-09
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-Ext2-FIPS-2023-04
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-Ext2-FIPS-PQ-2025-09
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-Ext1-FIPS-2023-04
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-Ext1-FIPS-PQ-2025-09
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-Ext0-FIPS-2023-04
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-Ext0-FIPS-PQ-2025-09
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-1-FIPS-2023-04
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-0-FIPS-2023-04
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-0-FIPS-PQ-2025-09
risk_reason: FIPS means the policy uses the AWS-LC FIPS validated cryptographic module, but it does not automatically mean the TLS configuration is strong. Ext/Ext0/Ext1/Ext2 and TLS13-1-2-FIPS policies include CBC/SHA or RSA key exchange compatibility ciphers; TLS13-1-1/1-0 FIPS policies are documented by AWS as legacy compatibility only.
security_value_reason: Use FIPS Res or TLS 1.3-only policies when FIPS is required and avoid FIPS policies that keep weak compatibility ciphers or TLS 1.0/1.1.
risk_basis:
Risk rule 1: cipher name does not contain ECDHE or TLS_, so it lacks forward secrecy (FS).
Risk rule 2: CBC mode, non-AEAD. AEAD examples are ChaCha20 and GCM. CBC has known attack history and is not recommended even when not fully broken in this context.
Risk rule 3: SHA means SHA-1 style legacy hash naming, not SHA256/SHA384/SHA512, and is treated as risky.
cipher_issue_examples: ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-Ext2-FIPS-2023-04 and ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-Ext2-FIPS-PQ-2025-09 include examples such as ECDHE-ECDSA-AES128-SHA256 (CBC), ECDHE-RSA-AES128-SHA256 (CBC), ECDHE-ECDSA-AES128-SHA (CBC,SHA), ECDHE-RSA-AES128-SHA (CBC,SHA), AES128-GCM-SHA256 (no ECDHE/TLS_, FS issue), AES128-SHA256 (CBC,FS issue), AES128-SHA (CBC,FS issue,SHA), AES256-GCM-SHA384 (FS issue), AES256-SHA256 (CBC,FS issue), AES256-SHA (CBC,FS issue,SHA).

recommended_values: ELBSecurityPolicy-FS-1-2-Res-2020-10
risky_values:
ELBSecurityPolicy-FS-1-2-Res-2019-08
ELBSecurityPolicy-FS-1-2-2019-08
ELBSecurityPolicy-FS-1-1-2019-08
ELBSecurityPolicy-FS-2018-06
risk_reason: Older FS policies may still include CBC/SHA compatibility ciphers or TLS 1.0/1.1. FS in the name does not remove all cipher-suite risk.
security_value_reason: Use the Res 2020 policy if constrained to the FS policy family; otherwise prefer current TLS 1.3/Res/PQ families.
risk_basis:
Risk rule 1: cipher name does not contain ECDHE or TLS_, so it lacks forward secrecy (FS).
Risk rule 2: CBC mode, non-AEAD. AEAD examples are ChaCha20 and GCM. CBC has known attack history and is not recommended even when not fully broken in this context.
Risk rule 3: SHA means SHA-1 style legacy hash naming, not SHA256/SHA384/SHA512, and is treated as risky.

recommended_values:
ACM managed certificate
Valid certificate chain and domain match
mTLS verify mode for client certificate authentication
risky_values:
Expired certificate
Domain mismatch
Incomplete certificate chain
mTLS verify without maintained trust store or CRL
risk_reason: TLS policy is not enough if certificate identity, validity, chain, or client-certificate trust is wrong.
security_value_reason: Use ACM where possible and maintain certificate renewal, SNI list, trust store, and revocation data.

recommended_values:
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-3-2021-06
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-3-PQ-2025-09
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-Res-2021-06
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-Res-PQ-2025-09
risky_values:
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-2021-06
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-PQ-2025-09
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-Ext2-2021-06
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-Ext2-PQ-2025-09
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-Ext1-2021-06
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-Ext1-PQ-2025-09
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-1-2021-06
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-0-2021-06
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-0-PQ-2025-09
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS-1-2-Ext-2018-06
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS-1-2-2017-01
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS-1-1-2017-01
ELBSecurityPolicy-2016-08
ELBSecurityPolicy-2015-05
risk_reason: Policies in the risk column either support TLS 1.0/1.1 or include weak compatibility ciphers by the same cipher rules used for ALB. NLB also has the older ELBSecurityPolicy-2015-05 policy.
security_value_reason: Use TLS 1.3-only or Res/PQ policies for TLS listeners. AWS recommends ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-Res-PQ-2025-09 or FIPS-PQ for PQ transition.
risk_basis:
Risk rule 1: cipher name does not contain ECDHE or TLS_, so it lacks forward secrecy (FS).
Risk rule 2: CBC mode, non-AEAD. AEAD examples are ChaCha20 and GCM. CBC has known attack history and is not recommended even when not fully broken in this context.
Risk rule 3: SHA means SHA-1 style legacy hash naming, not SHA256/SHA384/SHA512, and is treated as risky.

recommended_values:
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-3-FIPS-2023-04
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-3-FIPS-PQ-2025-09
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-Res-FIPS-2023-04

risky_values:
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-FIPS-2023-04
ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-FIPS-PQ-2025-09

risk_reason: FIPS policies use AWS-LC FIPS validated cryptographic module, but Ext/Ext0/Ext1/Ext2 and TLS13-1-2 FIPS families can still include CBC/SHA or RSA key exchange compatibility ciphers. TLS13-1-1/1-0 FIPS are legacy compatibility only.
security_value_reason: Use FIPS Res or TLS 1.3-only policies when FIPS is required.
risk_basis:
Risk rule 1: cipher name does not contain ECDHE or TLS_, so it lacks forward secrecy (FS).
Risk rule 2: CBC mode, non-AEAD. AEAD examples are ChaCha20 and GCM. CBC has known attack history and is not recommended even when not fully broken in this context.
Risk rule 3: SHA means SHA-1 style legacy hash naming, not SHA256/SHA384/SHA512, and is treated as risky.
cipher_issue_examples: ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-Ext2-FIPS-2023-04 and ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-Ext2-FIPS-PQ-2025-09 include examples such as ECDHE-ECDSA-AES128-SHA256 (CBC), ECDHE-RSA-AES128-SHA256 (CBC), ECDHE-ECDSA-AES128-SHA (CBC,SHA), ECDHE-RSA-AES128-SHA (CBC,SHA), AES128-GCM-SHA256 (no ECDHE/TLS_, FS issue), AES128-SHA256 (CBC,FS issue), AES128-SHA (CBC,FS issue,SHA), AES256-GCM-SHA384 (FS issue), AES256-SHA256 (CBC,FS issue), AES256-SHA (CBC,FS issue,SHA).

recommended_values: ELBSecurityPolicy-FS-1-2-Res-2020-10
risky_values:
ELBSecurityPolicy-FS-1-2-Res-2019-08
ELBSecurityPolicy-FS-1-2-2019-08
ELBSecurityPolicy-FS-1-1-2019-08
ELBSecurityPolicy-FS-2018-06
risk_reason: Older FS policies may include CBC/SHA ciphers or TLS 1.0/1.1.
security_value_reason: Use the Res 2020 policy if constrained to FS family; otherwise prefer current TLS 1.3/Res/PQ families.
risk_basis:
Risk rule 1: cipher name does not contain ECDHE or TLS_, so it lacks forward secrecy (FS).
Risk rule 2: CBC mode, non-AEAD. AEAD examples are ChaCha20 and GCM. CBC has known attack history and is not recommended even when not fully broken in this context.
Risk rule 3: SHA means SHA-1 style legacy hash naming, not SHA256/SHA384/SHA512, and is treated as risky.

recommended_values:
ACM certificate
TLS listener for load balancer TLS offload
TCP 443 pass-through when targets must decrypt TLS
HTTP2Preferred when HTTP/2 is required
risky_values:
IAM imported unsupported RSA key size such as RSA 4096
TCP listener assumed to terminate TLS
TLS offload when target-side end-to-end TLS is required
ALPN policy not aligned with client/backend protocol expectations
risk_reason: NLB TLS security depends on both the selected SslPolicy and certificate/protocol behavior. IAM-imported unsupported certificates can be rejected asynchronously and make a listener non-functional.
security_value_reason: Prefer ACM certificate validation and choose TLS offload versus TCP pass-through intentionally.

configuration_item: VPC Encryption Controls / Flow Logs encryption-status
recommended_values:
monitor mode for existing VPC first
enforce mode after remediation
Flow Logs with encryption-status, traffic-path, flow-direction
Transit Gateway encryption support where applicable
risky_values:
Encryption Controls off
Old Flow Logs without encryption-status
Enforce mode without remediation
Broad exclusions without compensating TLS/IPsec control
risk_reason: VPC is not a TLS policy service. The risk is missing visibility/enforcement for cleartext flows and unmanaged exclusions.
security_value_reason: Use monitor mode to discover cleartext paths, add encryption-status observability, remediate, and then enforce where appropriate.

configuration_item: DNSSEC signing / KSK / KMS / DS chain
recommended_values:
DNSSEC enabled for critical public hosted zones
KMS asymmetric customer managed key in us-east-1
KeySpec ECC_NIST_P256
DS record ECDSAP256SHA256 type 13 and SHA-256 digest type 2
CloudWatch alarms for DNSSECInternalFailure and DNSSECKeySigningKeysNeedingAction
risky_values:
DNSSEC disabled for critical public zone
Wrong KMS key spec, region, or key policy
KSK ACTION_NEEDED
Missing or wrong DS record
Missing DNSSEC alarms
risk_reason: DNSSEC failures are usually caused by chain-of-trust, KSK/KMS access, DS record, or monitoring gaps rather than cipher-suite selection.
security_value_reason: Use Route 53 DNSSEC signing with correct KMS/KSK requirements and establish the DS chain of trust before relying on DNSSEC validation.

recommended_values: ikev2
risky_values:
ikev1 retained as long-term baseline
default permits both ikev1 and ikev2 if not narrowed
risk_reason: IKEv1 is a legacy compatibility option. AWS defaults include both ikev1 and ikev2 unless narrowed.
security_value_reason: Prefer IKEv2 and remove unused legacy IKE versions from permitted tunnel options.

recommended_values:
AES128-GCM-16
AES256-GCM-16
risky_values:
AES128 used as long-term compatibility instead of GCM
AES256 used as long-term compatibility instead of GCM
risk_reason: AWS supports AES128/AES256 and AES-GCM options. GCM provides AEAD; non-GCM AES should be treated as compatibility when GCM is available.
security_value_reason: Prefer AES-GCM for authenticated encryption in both IKE phase 1 and phase 2 where the customer gateway supports it.

risky_values: SHA1
risk_reason: AWS defaults include SHA1 together with SHA2 values. SHA1 should not remain a preferred or long-term integrity baseline.
security_value_reason: Use SHA2 integrity algorithms and remove SHA1 unless a temporary customer gateway compatibility exception is documented.

recommended_values:
Group 14 or higher
Prefer groups 19, 20, 21 where supported
risky_values:
Group 2
Group 5
risk_reason: AWS defaults include group 2 for phase 1 and group 5 for phase 2. These are weaker legacy DH groups.
security_value_reason: Use stronger DH groups and align both customer gateway and AWS tunnel options.

recommended_values:
PSK stored in AWS Secrets Manager
Private certificate from AWS Private CA where PSK is not desired
PSK rotation and customer gateway configuration governance
risky_values:
PSK stored directly in Site-to-Site VPN service without enhanced storage
Weak or shared PSK lifecycle
Certificate chain or X.509 extension validation problems
risk_reason: PSK is the default authentication option. AWS also supports Secrets Manager storage and private certificates from AWS Private CA.
security_value_reason: Use Secrets Manager for PSK storage where possible, or private certificate authentication with valid certificate chain governance.

recommended_values:
TLS 1.2 or later
Current AWS provided client or patched OpenVPN-based client
risky_values:
Assuming user-selectable TLS security policy exists
Old or unpatched client software
Nonstandard client configuration that weakens verification
risk_reason: Client VPN provides TLS 1.2 or later, but users do not manage an ELB-style security policy. Risk is mainly endpoint/client certificate and client configuration governance.
security_value_reason: Keep clients current and avoid treating Client VPN as a cipher-policy service.

recommended_values:
ACM server certificate
RFC 5280-compliant certificates
RSA 2048-bit certificate baseline
Mutual authentication when certificate-based client identity is required
risky_values:
Expired server or client certificate
RSA 1024-bit certificate as long-term baseline
Client certificate without CN attribute
Client private key distribution not controlled
risk_reason: AWS Client VPN requires a server certificate in ACM. Mutual authentication uses client certificates. Client VPN supports 1024-bit and 2048-bit RSA key sizes only, but 1024-bit should be treated as weak for long-term use.
security_value_reason: Use ACM-managed server certificate lifecycle and controlled client certificate/private-key distribution.

recommended_values:
Valid and monitored CRL when mutual authentication revocation is used
Process for revoking per-user client certificates
risky_values:
Expired CRL
No revocation process for issued client certificates
Shared client certificate/key across users
risk_reason: AWS troubleshooting docs identify expired CRL as a cause of TLS handshake failures. Shared or unmanaged client certificates also weaken certificate-based authentication.
security_value_reason: Use per-client certificates where practical and keep CRL lifecycle monitored.

recommended_values:
Use HTTPS/TLS for AWS API access
Use private DNS namespace for internal-only discovery
Use Route 53 DNSSEC governance for public DNS integrity requirements
Enable TLS on downstream service endpoints
risky_values:
Treating Cloud Map namespace as TLS termination or encryption boundary
Public DNS namespace used for integrity-sensitive discovery without DNSSEC governance
Sensitive secrets placed in instance attributes, names, or tags
Downstream service endpoint exposed without TLS
risk_reason: Cloud Map maps names to resources and can create Route 53 hosted zones, but it does not provide user-selectable TLS/cipher/key configuration for the discovered service traffic.
security_value_reason: Keep Cloud Map as discovery metadata; enforce encryption in the AWS API path, DNS integrity layer, and downstream service endpoint.

recommended_values:
SYMMETRIC_DEFAULT + ENCRYPT_DECRYPT for AWS integrated service encryption
RSA_3072/RSA_4096 or ECC_NIST_P256/P384/P521 for asymmetric sign/verify or encrypt/decrypt use cases as appropriate
HMAC_256/HMAC_384/HMAC_512 for MAC use cases
ML_DSA_44 / ML_DSA_65 / ML_DSA_87 for post-quantum digital signature use cases
risky_values:
KeySpec and KeyUsage mismatch
Asymmetric or HMAC KMS key used for AWS service encryption that requires symmetric KMS keys
RSAES_OAEP_SHA_1 when SHA-256 OAEP is available
HMAC_224 as a weak baseline
One KMS key reused across incompatible purposes
risk_reason: AWS KMS key spec and key usage cannot be changed after creation. AWS integrated services use symmetric encryption KMS keys. Each KMS key has one key usage.
security_value_reason: Choose key spec and key usage from the business purpose first; use SYMMETRIC_DEFAULT for most AWS service encryption and ML-DSA only for PQ signature requirements.

recommended_values:
Origin AWS_KMS for standard managed key material
MultiRegion true only for cross-region cryptographic portability requirements
AWS_CLOUDHSM or EXTERNAL only with documented operational need
risky_values:
EXTERNAL imported key material without expiration, availability, and rotation process
Custom key store or external key store without availability/SLA governance
Multi-Region key used where data residency or blast radius should remain regional
risk_reason: Origin and MultiRegion cannot be changed after key creation. Imported or custom key store material shifts operational responsibilities to the customer.
security_value_reason: Use AWS_KMS origin by default and document operational controls when choosing imported material, custom key stores, external key stores, or multi-Region keys.

api_endpoint: EnableKeyRotation / RotateKeyOnDemand / manual rotation with new KMS key and alias migration
recommended_values:
Automatic rotation for eligible symmetric AWS_KMS customer managed keys
Manual rotation plan for asymmetric, HMAC, imported, and custom key store keys
Rotation monitoring in CloudTrail/EventBridge/CloudWatch
risky_values:
No rotation policy for customer managed keys
Assuming automatic rotation works for asymmetric, HMAC, imported, or custom key store keys
Deleting old key before all ciphertext/signature validation dependencies are migrated
risk_reason: Automatic rotation applies only to eligible symmetric encryption KMS keys with AWS_KMS origin. Asymmetric, HMAC, imported, and custom key store keys require manual rotation patterns.
security_value_reason: Enable automatic rotation where eligible and define manual rotation for non-eligible key types.

recommended_values:
BypassPolicyLockoutSafetyCheck=false
Least-privilege key administrators and key users
Use kms:KeySpec, kms:KeyUsage, kms:EncryptionAlgorithm, kms:SigningAlgorithm, kms:MacAlgorithm condition keys where appropriate
risky_values:
BypassPolicyLockoutSafetyCheck=true without explicit recovery path
Overly broad key administrators or key users
No algorithm restrictions where only specific algorithms are approved
risk_reason: AWS warns that bypassing the policy lockout safety check increases the risk that the KMS key becomes unmanageable.
security_value_reason: Keep the safety check enabled and use key policy/IAM conditions to restrict key creation and cryptographic operations.

recommended_values:
FIPS mode for FIPS 140 requirement
hsm2m.medium only when non-FIPS mode is explicitly needed
mode selected before production because it cannot be changed after creation
risky_values:
non-FIPS mode selected for regulated workloads requiring FIPS validated algorithms
assuming a cluster mode can be changed after creation
backup restore across incompatible FIPS/non-FIPS modes
risk_reason: CloudHSM cluster mode is selected at creation and cannot be changed later. Non-FIPS mode allows non-FIPS-approved algorithms.
security_value_reason: Choose FIPS mode when compliance requires FIPS validated keys and algorithms; use non-FIPS only with documented algorithm need.

api_endpoint: Client SDK mechanism selection: PKCS#11 / JCE / CNG / KSP
recommended_values:
AES-GCM
AES Key Wrap
RSA-OAEP with SHA-2
ECDSA with SHA-2
HMAC with SHA-2
ECDH with SHA-2 KDF where applicable
risky_values:
DES3-CBC / DES3-ECB as new baseline
SHA1 digest/signature/KDF as new baseline
RSA-PKCS / PKCS#1 v1.5 padding for new encryption/signature baseline
AES-ECB
risk_reason: CloudHSM supports many mechanisms for compatibility. Supported does not mean recommended for new designs.
security_value_reason: Use modern AEAD, SHA-2, and OAEP/ECDSA/HMAC mechanisms and keep legacy mechanisms as documented compatibility exceptions.

api_endpoint: CloudHSM CLI / PKCS#11 key attributes / JCE/CNG provider key attributes
recommended_values:
least-privilege key attributes
non-extractable private keys by default
CU ownership and role separation
key wrapping only with trusted wrapping keys
risky_values:
extractable sensitive keys without documented need
overbroad encrypt/decrypt/sign/wrap/unwrap attributes
shared CU credentials
key owner lifecycle not documented
risk_reason: CloudHSM key owners and attributes govern how keys can be used or exported. Overbroad attributes defeat HSM isolation goals.
security_value_reason: Restrict key attributes to intended operations and govern CU ownership, wrapping, and deletion lifecycle.

recommended_values:
RSA_2048
EC_prime256v1
EC_secp384r1
CertificateTransparencyLoggingPreference=ENABLED
DNS validation for public certificates
risky_values:
RSA_1024 as new baseline
CertificateTransparencyLoggingPreference=DISABLED without privacy/legal justification
certificate domain/SAN mismatch
private certificate algorithm family mismatch with CA key
risk_reason: ACM public certificate requests support RSA_2048, EC_prime256v1, and EC_secp384r1. CT logging helps detect mistaken or malicious public certificate issuance.
security_value_reason: Use ACM-managed public certificates with DNS validation and CT logging enabled unless there is a documented exception.

recommended_values:
complete PEM certificate chain
private key matches certificate public key
expiration monitoring for imported certificates
prefer ACM-issued managed certificates when possible
risky_values:
imported certificate expected to renew automatically
encrypted private key submitted for import
private key mismatch
incomplete chain
expired or not-yet-valid certificate
risk_reason: ACM does not provide managed renewal for imported certificates. Import requires a matching unencrypted private key and valid PEM material.
security_value_reason: Use imported certificates only when necessary and add renewal, chain, and private-key handling controls.

configuration_item: ExportCertificate / exportable public certificate private key handling
recommended_values:
export only when certificate must be deployed outside integrated AWS services
passphrase supplied from a protected file or secret process
post-export private key storage and revocation process
risky_values:
unnecessary export of private key material
weak or inline passphrase
passphrase committed to scripts/logs/shell history
exported private key without inventory or revocation plan
risk_reason: ExportCertificate returns certificate, chain, and encrypted private key. Export expands the private-key trust boundary beyond ACM.
security_value_reason: Use ACM-integrated deployment when possible; if export is required, protect passphrase and private key lifecycle.

recommended_values:
AWSLambda-SHA384-ECDSA
Notation-OCI-SHA384-ECDSA
AWSIoTDeviceManagement-SHA256-ECDSA
AmazonFreeRTOS-Default
risky_values:
AmazonFreeRTOS-TI-CC3220SF as new baseline because it is SHA1-RSA
wrong platformId for artifact type
IoT workflow certificate imported into ACM without lifecycle controls
risk_reason: Signer platformId determines the signature algorithm and format. AWS documents AmazonFreeRTOS-TI-CC3220SF as SHA1-RSA, which should be treated as a legacy compatibility platform.
security_value_reason: Use SHA256/ECDSA or SHA384/ECDSA platforms where supported and match platformId to artifact type.

api_endpoint: PutSigningProfile.signatureValidityPeriod / IAM permissions for Signer profiles
recommended_values:
shortest practical signature validity period for Lambda/container workflows
least-privilege StartSigningJob and profile administration
profile version governance and revocation/replacement process
risky_values:
maximum validity used by default without lifecycle rationale
overbroad signing permissions
uncontrolled profile reuse across environments
no revocation or replacement process after signing material compromise
risk_reason: Long-lived signing validity and broad signing permissions increase blast radius if a profile, artifact pipeline, or signing material is compromised.
security_value_reason: Treat signing profiles as sensitive cryptographic control points with scoped IAM and lifecycle governance.

api_endpoint: AWS Encryption SDK client configuration: commitmentPolicy / keyring / discoveryFilter
recommended_values:
RequireEncryptRequireDecrypt
AWS KMS keyring with explicit KMS key identifiers
discovery keyring only with discoveryFilter
encryption context with non-secret integrity metadata
risky_values:
ForbidEncryptAllowDecrypt used as steady-state new encryption baseline
discovery keyring without discoveryFilter
raw keyring without strong key storage controls
missing encryption context for data binding where needed
risk_reason: AWS recommends key commitment as a best practice. Discovery keyrings without filters can attempt keys across accounts/Regions and widen the decryption surface.
security_value_reason: Use commitment policy explicitly, prefer AWS KMS keyrings, and restrict discovery mode.

api_endpoint: AWS Database Encryption SDK table encryption config / cryptographic actions / beacon configuration / KMS hierarchical keyring
recommended_values:
ENCRYPT_AND_SIGN for sensitive fields
SIGN_ONLY only for integrity-only fields
AWS KMS Hierarchical keyring for searchable encryption
beacon design before writing production data
risky_values:
DO_NOTHING for sensitive fields
changing cryptographic action on existing populated fields
beacon configured on DO_NOTHING field
beacon length/design that leaks too much plaintext structure
risk_reason: Cryptographic actions determine field protection. Beacons are HMAC-derived searchable tags and are not encrypted; they must be designed carefully before data is written.
security_value_reason: Use encrypt/sign actions deliberately and plan searchable encryption with the hierarchical keyring and leakage analysis.

api_endpoint: Amazon S3 Encryption Client builder keyring / wrapping key / commitmentPolicy / legacy wrapping algorithms
recommended_values:
AWS KMS wrapping key where possible
AES-GCM object encryption
RequireEncryptRequireDecrypt or migration-appropriate require policy
current fully supported wrapping algorithms
risky_values:
legacy wrapping algorithms enabled for new encryption
raw AES/RSA wrapping keys without HSM/KMS-grade protection
commitment policy allowing non-committed encryption as steady state
instruction file handling not governed
risk_reason: S3 Encryption Client depends on wrapping key protection and commitment policy. Legacy wrapping algorithms are for decrypting older data, not new encryption baselines.
security_value_reason: Use current client versions, modern commitment policy, and KMS/HSM-protected wrapping keys.

recommended_values:
AES_128 / AES_192 / AES_256 where supported by the payment key type
TDES_3KEY only when TDES interoperability is required
KeyUsage scoped to the payment purpose, such as TR31_P0_PIN_ENCRYPTION_KEY, TR31_C0_CARD_VERIFICATION_KEY, TR31_D0_SYMMETRIC_DATA_ENCRYPTION_KEY, TR31_M6_CMAC_KEY, TR31_M7_HMAC_KEY
KeyModesOfUse limited to required operations only
risky_values:
TDES_2KEY as new baseline
NoRestrictions for production keys without strict justification
KeyUsage reused across PIN, CVV, MAC, and data encryption purposes
Encrypt/Decrypt/Wrap/Unwrap all enabled when only one operation is required
TR31_K0 where TR31_K1 key block protection key is recommended
risk_reason: AWS Payment Cryptography binds key algorithm, usage, and modes of use to keys. Valid does not always mean least privilege or suitable as a new baseline. TDES_2KEY and NoRestrictions should be treated as high-risk compatibility choices.
security_value_reason: Scope each key to a single payment purpose, choose AES where supported, use TDES only for payment interoperability requirements, and minimize KeyModesOfUse.

configuration_item: Key import/export method and key block format
recommended_values:
TR-34 for initial KEK exchange where supported
ECDH for AES-256 key exchange needs where supported
TR-31 for working key exchange after KEK establishment
RSA wrap only as fallback when TR-34 is not available
KEK rotation and crypto-period governance
risky_values:
paper key components as long-term operating model
RSA wrap used when TR-34 is available and required integrity metadata is needed
TR-31 Version A/C accepted from legacy sources without migration plan
exportable working keys without business need
wrapping key weaker than protected key
risk_reason: AWS recommends standards-based electronic key exchange and notes TR-34 for KEK exchange, TR-31 for working key exchange, and RSA wrap as a fallback with limitations. Export expands the key transport boundary.
security_value_reason: Use standards-based key blocks and ensure wrapping keys are at least as strong as protected keys; document KEK crypto periods and export need.

configuration_item: Payment data operations: PIN / CVV / MAC / data encryption
api_endpoint: Data Plane operations: EncryptData / DecryptData / GenerateMac / VerifyMac / GenerateCardValidationData / VerifyAuthRequestCryptogram / TranslatePinData
recommended_values:
Dedicated key usage for each operation family
CMAC/HMAC keys for MAC where supported by scheme
DUKPT BDK/IPEK with scoped derivation use
separate keys for test and production environments
CloudTrail/IAM controls for key use operations
risky_values:
same key reused across PIN, CVV, MAC, and data encryption
NoRestrictions used to bypass operation scoping
test keys or aliases used in production
operation permissions granted broadly without purpose separation
sensitive data in tags, aliases, or free-form fields
risk_reason: Payment cryptography operations are strongly tied to key usage. Reusing one key across payment functions or environments weakens auditability and can violate payment key-separation expectations.
security_value_reason: Separate keys, aliases, IAM permissions, and environments by payment operation and business role.

configuration_item: HSM protection and TLS/PQ API transport
api_endpoint: AWS Payment Cryptography service endpoints / VPC endpoints / HSM boundary
recommended_values:
PCI PTS HSM and FIPS 140-2 Level 3 service protection
TLS 1.2-1.3 with strong cryptography
hybrid post-quantum TLS support for API endpoints
PrivateLink VPC endpoint where private connectivity is required
risky_values:
assuming exported key material can leave in cleartext
API traffic without modern TLS client support
public endpoint used where private connectivity is mandated
credentials or secrets in URL, tags, aliases, or free-form fields
risk_reason: AWS states key material never leaves the HSM boundary in plaintext and API calls require TLS. Customers still control API clients, IAM, endpoint choice, and sensitive metadata hygiene.
security_value_reason: Use modern TLS clients, PrivateLink where required, and keep sensitive values out of metadata fields.
